<title>Functor of points — schemes through their $R$-valued points</title>

<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  .two-col{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:.8rem}
  @media (max-width:720px){ .two-col{grid-template-columns:1fr} }
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 The reframe 2 Affine example 3 Representability 4 $\mathrm{GL}_n$ 5 Moduli 6 Why it fails 7 Preview: stacks 8 Yoneda embedding 9 Base change

The functor of points

A scheme is what it does to rings. Instead of gluing local affines, think of a scheme $X$ as the rule $R \mapsto X(R)$ sending a commutative ring to the set of its $R$-points. This is the change of posture that makes moduli problems natural — and points directly at stacks.

blocks.html:
1. The reframe

You already have two pictures of a scheme $X$:

  • a locally ringed space $(|X|, \mathcal{O}_X)$ glued from spectra;
  • the category object $X \in \mathsf{Sch}$, determined up to isomorphism by its morphisms in and out.

The second picture, pushed hard, becomes the functor of points. The Yoneda lemma says the hom-functor $$h_X = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(-, X) : \mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}$$ determines $X$ up to unique isomorphism. Equivalently, by adjunction $\operatorname{Spec} \dashv \Gamma$, we may restrict this functor to affine test objects and regard $X$ as a covariant functor on commutative rings:

$$X \ \leftrightarrow\ h_X\big|_{\mathrm{CRing}} : \mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}, \qquad R \mapsto X(R) := \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(\operatorname{Spec} R,\, X).$$
Why this is a reframe, not a repackaging. The usual picture asks "what are the solutions of these equations in a fixed ring $k$?" The functor-of-points picture asks "what is the rule that takes any ring $R$ and returns the set of $R$-solutions?" The solution rule — with its naturality in $R$ — is the scheme.

A morphism of schemes $X \to Y$ is, in this language, a natural transformation $h_X \Rightarrow h_Y$, i.e. a family of set maps $X(R) \to Y(R)$ compatible with ring homs $R \to R'$.

Two reasons to bother:

  1. Moduli problems arrive as functors. "Elliptic curves over $R$," "vector bundles on $S$ of rank $n$," "subschemes of $\mathbb{P}^n$ of Hilbert polynomial $P$" — each is naturally a functor $\mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}$ or into groupoids. Asking whether it's a scheme means asking whether the functor is representable.
  2. Constructions become algebra. Fibered products, group schemes, affine bundles, loop spaces — each is trivial to describe at the level of $R$-points, then lifted by representability.

Two postures on $V(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$
hover / click a ring
select a ring to compare the two viewpoints
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#compare-svg'), out = $('#compare-out');
  const RINGS = [
    {k:'Z', name:'\u2124'},
    {k:'Q', name:'\u211A'},
    {k:'R', name:'\u211D'},
    {k:'F2', name:'\ud835\udd3d\u2082'},
    {k:'F3', name:'\ud835\udd3d\u2083'},
    {k:'F5', name:'\ud835\udd3d\u2085'},
    {k:'F7', name:'\ud835\udd3d\u2087'}
  ];
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:20,y:20,width:260,height:180,rx:10,
      fill:'#11151d',stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    const t1 = SVG('text',{x:150,y:50,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t1.textContent = 'OLD: fix a ring';
    svg.appendChild(t1);
    const t1b = SVG('text',{x:150,y:80,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14,fill:'#fff'});
    t1b.textContent = 'X(R) for one chosen R';
    svg.appendChild(t1b);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:360,y:20,width:260,height:180,rx:10,
      fill:'#11151d',stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    const t2 = SVG('text',{x:490,y:50,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t2.textContent = 'NEW: vary the ring';
    svg.appendChild(t2);
    const t2b = SVG('text',{x:490,y:80,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':14,fill:'#fff'});
    t2b.textContent = 'the functor R \u21a6 X(R)';
    svg.appendChild(t2b);
    RINGS.forEach((r,i)=>{
      const x = 380 + (i%4)*55;
      const y = 120 + Math.floor(i/4)*40;
      const g = SVG('g',{style:'cursor:pointer'});
      const rect = SVG('rect',{x:x-22,y:y-14,width:44,height:26,rx:6,
        fill:'var(--panel2)',stroke:'var(--line)','data-k':r.k});
      const lab = SVG('text',{x,y:y+5,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
      lab.textContent = r.name;
      g.appendChild(rect); g.appendChild(lab);
      g.addEventListener('click', ()=> show(r));
      svg.appendChild(g);
    });
    const arr = SVG('text',{x:320,y:115,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':24,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
    arr.textContent = '\u27fa';
    svg.appendChild(arr);
    const sub1 = SVG('text',{x:150,y:120,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    sub1.textContent = 'a single set of solutions';
    svg.appendChild(sub1);
    const sub2 = SVG('text',{x:150,y:170,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    sub2.textContent = '(e.g. rational points)';
    svg.appendChild(sub2);
  }
  function show(r){
    const counts = count(r.k);
    out.textContent = `X(${r.name}) = { (a,b) in ${r.name}^2 : b^2 = a^3 + 1 }` +
      (counts.finite ? `    |X(${r.name})| = ${counts.size}` : '    (infinite; try a finite field)');
  }
  function count(k){
    if(k==='Z' || k==='Q') return {finite:false};
    if(k==='R') return {finite:false};
    const p = +k.slice(1);
    let n = 0;
    for(let a=0;a<p;a++) for(let b=0;b<p;b++){
      if(((b*b - a*a*a - 1) % p + p) % p === 0) n++;
    }
    return {finite:true, size:n};
  }
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
See also
  • Yoneda, limits, and adjunctions
  • Morphisms of schemes
Used in
Functor of points · Base change as pullback of functors
Functor of points · Representability and moduli functors
Functor of points · The Yoneda embedding for schemes
</section>

2. Worked example: an affine cubic

Let $X = \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2 - x^3 - 1)$. Because $X$ is affine, its $R$-points are spectacularly concrete:

$$X(R) \;=\; \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(y^2-x^3-1),\, R) \;=\; \{(a,b) \in R^2 : b^2 = a^3 + 1\}.$$

So the functor $X$ is the solution-set functor of the affine equation $y^2 = x^3 + 1$. Two examples you can verify by hand:

  • $X(\mathbb{F}_2) = \{(0,1),(1,0)\}$. Two points.
  • $X(\mathbb{F}_3) = \{(0,1),(0,2),(2,0),(2,1),(2,2)\}$. Five points. Wait, check $(2,1)$: $1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \equiv 0 \not= 1$. Retract that. The widget below does the brute-force properly.
The point. As the ring $R$ varies, $X(R)$ is literally the set of solutions in $R$. There is no hidden infrastructure: the scheme is this rule, and a morphism $X \to Y$ is a rule that turns $R$-solutions of one equation into $R$-solutions of the other, naturally in $R$.

params.title: W1 · $R$-points of $y^2 = x^3 + 1$
params.controlsLiteral:

blocks.html: Experiment a bit: the count $|X(\mathbb{F}_p)|$ fluctuates as a function of $p$ in a way that encodes deep arithmetic (this curve has $L$-function tied to a modular form). All of that is hiding inside the functor — you cannot see it by looking at the space $|X|$.

3. Representable functors

A functor $F : \mathrm{CRing} \to \mathsf{Set}$ is representable if there is a scheme $X$ with a natural isomorphism $F \cong h_X$. When $X$ is affine, $X = \operatorname{Spec} A$ for some ring $A$, and Yoneda plus the $\operatorname{Spec} \dashv \Gamma$ adjunction collapses to:

$$F \text{ representable by } \operatorname{Spec} A \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F(R) \cong \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{CRing}}(A, R) \text{ naturally in } R.$$

That is: representing a set-valued functor on rings means writing its value on $R$ as ring homs out of a fixed ring $A$. The universal element of $F$ is the image of $\mathrm{id}_A$; every other element of $F(R)$ is obtained by "plugging" the universal element into the map $A \to R$.

Example. The affine line $\mathbb{A}^1$. As a functor, $\mathbb{A}^1(R) = R$ (the underlying set of $R$). Representable by $\mathbb{Z}[t]$: a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}[t] \to R$ is just a choice of element of $R$.
Example. The multiplicative group $\mathbb{G}_m$. $\mathbb{G}_m(R) = R^\times$. Representable by $\mathbb{Z}[t, t^{-1}]$: forcing $t$ to become a unit.

blocks.html:
Representability is a theorem, not a definition: you exhibit the pair $(A, \xi)$ where $\xi \in F(A)$ is universal, and then verify that "map out of $A$" is really the same as "element of $F(R)$."

Yoneda uniqueness. If both $X$ and $Y$ represent $F$, then $h_X \cong h_Y$, hence $X \cong Y$ in $\mathsf{Sch}$ by Yoneda. The representing scheme is unique up to unique isomorphism.

Used in
Functor of points · When functors require stacks
Moduli spaces · Moduli problems and parameters

4. $\mathrm{GL}_n$ as a functor, first

The cleanest advertisement for the functor-of-points posture is $\mathrm{GL}_n$. Define it by what it does to rings:

$$\mathrm{GL}_n(R) \;=\; \{\,n\times n \text{ matrices } M \text{ with entries in } R \text{ such that } \det(M) \in R^\times\,\}.$$

This is clearly a functor (ring maps induce matrix maps preserving units). Is it representable? Yes — by

$$\mathrm{GL}_n \;\cong\; \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x_{11}, \ldots, x_{nn}, t] \big/ (t \cdot \det(X) - 1).$$

The variable $t$ is a formal inverse of the determinant; forcing $t \cdot \det = 1$ makes $\det$ a unit. Note how much less painful this is than trying to describe $\mathrm{GL}_n$ as a locally ringed space first.

blocks.html: Everything works the same way for $\mathrm{SL}_n$, $\mathrm{O}_n$, $\mathrm{Sp}_{2n}$: describe the group of $R$-points as a set of matrices cut out by polynomial conditions; write down the representing ring by adjoining formal inverses of determinant-type functions. That's it.

blocks.html: </section>

5. Moduli problems as functors

The functor-of-points language is most useful when the functor is natural but the space isn't yet constructed. A moduli problem is exactly this: define a functor whose $R$-points are "families of objects of type $T$ over $\operatorname{Spec} R$, up to isomorphism," then ask whether it's representable.

The testbed is the moduli of elliptic curves $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$:

$$\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(R) \;=\; \left\{ \begin{array}{c} \text{elliptic curves } E/R \\ \text{(smooth proper genus-1 with a section)} \end{array} \right\} \Big/ \cong_R.$$

Over an algebraically closed field, elliptic curves are classified up to isomorphism by the $j$-invariant, so one hopes $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ should "be" $\mathbb{A}^1_j$. Over a general ring the situation is subtler, but it is irresistible to slide a parameter and watch a family of curves.

W5 · family $y^2 = x^3 + tx + 1$
scrub $t$; see the curve and $j$-invariant
t = 0 j = 0 Δ = ?
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#fam-svg'), out = $('#fam-out');
  const slider = $('#fam-t'), tv=$('#fam-tv'), jv=$('#fam-jv'), dv=$('#fam-disc');
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const t = +slider.value;
    const a = t, b = 1;
    const disc = -16*(4*a*a*a + 27*b*b);
    const j = Math.abs(disc) < 1e-9 ? null : -1728 * (4*a*a*a) * 64 / disc;
    tv.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`;
    jv.textContent = j===null ? 'j = singular' : `j = ${j.toFixed(2)}`;
    dv.textContent = `\u0394 = ${disc.toFixed(2)}`;
    // axes
    const cx=320, cy=130, sx=50, sy=50;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:40,y1:cy,x2:600,y2:cy,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:cx,y1:20,x2:cx,y2:240,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    for(let i=-5;i<=5;i++){
      const tx = SVG('text',{x:cx+i*sx/2,y:cy+14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':9,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      tx.textContent = (i/2).toFixed(1); svg.appendChild(tx);
    }
    const singular = Math.abs(disc) < 1e-2;
    function drawSeg(pts){
      if(pts.length < 2) return;
      const d = 'M ' + pts.map(p=>p.join(' ')).join(' L ');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d, stroke: singular?'var(--pink)':'var(--yellow)', 'stroke-width':2, fill:'none'}));
    }
    // plot y^2 = x^3 + t*x + 1 for x in range
    const ptsU = [], ptsL = [];
    for(let xv=-3; xv<=3; xv+=0.01){
      const rhs = xv*xv*xv + a*xv + b;
      if(rhs < 0) { if(ptsU.length){ drawSeg(ptsU); ptsU.length=0; } continue; }
      const yv = Math.sqrt(rhs);
      ptsU.push([cx+xv*sx, cy - yv*sy]);
      ptsL.push([cx+xv*sx, cy + yv*sy]);
    }
    drawSeg(ptsU);
    drawSeg(ptsL);
    const cap = SVG('text',{x:500, y:40, 'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,
      fill: singular ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--green)'});
    cap.textContent = singular ? 'singular fiber: node/cusp (curve degenerates)' : 'smooth elliptic curve';
    svg.appendChild(cap);
    out.textContent = `E_t : y\u00b2 = x\u00b3 + (${t.toFixed(2)})x + 1\n` +
      `discriminant \u0394 = -16(4t\u00b3 + 27)\n` +
      `j(E_t) = -1728 \u00b7 (4t)\u00b3 / \u0394   (when \u0394 \u2260 0)\n` +
      (singular ? 'at \u0394 = 0 the family degenerates; this point is missing from \ud835\udcdc_{1,1}.' :
        'smooth elliptic curve \u2014 well-defined isomorphism class.');
  }
  slider.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Imagine repeating this for every commutative ring $R$. The functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ sends $R$ to isomorphism classes of elliptic curves over $R$. This looks good: sending a parameter family over $\operatorname{Spec} R$ to its isomorphism class is clearly functorial in $R$. The shock is that $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is not representable as a scheme.

6. Why representability fails

The obstruction is automorphisms. A generic elliptic curve has only the automorphism $[-1]$ (order 2). But certain curves have extra automorphisms:

  • $y^2 = x^3 + x$ has $\mathrm{Aut} \cong \mathbb{Z}/4$ (coming from $x \mapsto -x$, $y \mapsto \pm i y$), over any field containing $i$.
  • $y^2 = x^3 + 1$ has $\mathrm{Aut} \cong \mathbb{Z}/6$ (via $x \mapsto \zeta_3 x$), over any field containing $\zeta_3$.

Here is the precise form of the obstruction. Suppose $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ were representable, say $\mathcal{M}_{1,1} \cong h_M$ for a scheme $M$. Then for any scheme $S$ and any family of elliptic curves $\mathcal{E} \to S$, there would be a unique classifying morphism $S \to M$. In particular, given an isomorphism class of family $[\mathcal{E}]$, the family itself would be reconstructible up to isomorphism from its classifying map.

It isn't. You can glue two families along different isomorphisms of their fibers and get non-isomorphic total spaces with the same "pointwise" classifying data — a non-trivial twist by a $\mathrm{Aut}$-valued cocycle.

W6 · the automorphism twist
two families over a 2-point base
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#tw-svg'), out = $('#tw-out');
  const btns = $$('#w-twist button[data-iso]');
  let iso = 'id';
  btns.forEach(b=>b.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    btns.forEach(x=>x.classList.remove('active')); b.classList.add('active');
    iso = b.dataset.iso; render();
  }));
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    // Base: two points p0, p1
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:60,y1:240,x2:580,y2:240,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    const basePts = [ [180, 240], [460, 240] ];
    basePts.forEach((p,i)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p[0],cy:p[1],r:6,fill:'var(--blue)'}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:p[0], y:p[1]+22,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = i===0 ? 'p\u2080' : 'p\u2081'; svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    const caption = SVG('text',{x:320, y:262, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    caption.textContent = 'base S (two-point scheme, e.g. Spec k \u2294 Spec k)';
    svg.appendChild(caption);
    // Two fibers above each base point
    const fiberX = (x, label, color)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x,y1:200,x2:x,y2:40,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-dasharray':'2 4'}));
      // draw a schematic elliptic curve oval
      svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:x, cy:115, rx:32, ry:60, fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.1)', stroke:color, 'stroke-width':1.5}));
      const lab = SVG('text',{x, y:40, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11, fill:color});
      lab.textContent = label; svg.appendChild(lab);
    };
    // Family A: same E at both base points, glued by identity
    // Family B: same E, glued by [-1]
    fiberX(180, 'E', 'var(--yellow)');
    fiberX(460, 'E', 'var(--yellow)');
    // A small marked point on each fiber to show the gluing
    // on p0: mark a point at "north"; on p1: mark based on iso
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:180, cy:70, r:4, fill:'var(--pink)'}));
    const p2y = (iso==='id') ? 70 : 160; // identity: north; [-1]: south (the image under hyperelliptic involution)
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:460, cy:p2y, r:4, fill:'var(--pink)'}));
    // Gluing arc
    drawArrow(svg, [186, 70], [454, p2y], {color:'var(--cyan)', curve:-30, markerId:'tw-arr',
      label: iso==='id' ? 'glue by id' : 'glue by [-1]', width:1.8});
    const kind = SVG('text',{x:320, y:25, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13, fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    kind.textContent = iso==='id' ? 'trivial family: E \u00d7 S' :
      'twisted family: same fibers, different gluing';
    svg.appendChild(kind);
    out.textContent = iso==='id'
      ? 'Family A = E \u00d7 S: fibers E over both p\u2080 and p\u2081, identified canonically.\n' +
        'isomorphism class at each fiber: [E].'
      : 'Family B: fibers E over both points, but the total space is glued via the nontrivial automorphism [-1].\n' +
        'It has the same pointwise isomorphism classes as A, but A \u2262 B as families.\n' +
        'If \ud835\udcdc_{1,1} were representable, each family would have a unique classifying map S\u2192M, \n' +
        'so A and B would give the same map \u21d2 A \u2245 B. Contradiction. Hence \ud835\udcdc_{1,1} is NOT representable.';
  }
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The sheaf-of-sets argument also fails. One might hope to fix this by sheafifying the presheaf of isomorphism classes. But the isomorphism-class assignment is not a sheaf: for a cover $\{U_i \to S\}$, specifying a compatible family of classes on each $U_i$ does not uniquely assemble to a class on $S$ — the gluing requires remembering the automorphisms you used to identify overlaps. Sheafification literally forgets this information, producing a functor that no longer captures the moduli problem.

This is the content of the slogan "moduli of objects with automorphisms is not a scheme." The presence of $\mathrm{Aut} \neq 1$ on some fibers is not a nuisance to be quotiented away; it is structural data that the functor-of-points-to-SETS cannot remember.

Contrast with a rigidified problem: elliptic curves with full level-$N$ structure (a basis for the $N$-torsion) for $N \ge 3$ has no automorphisms (over a base where $N$ is invertible), and the corresponding moduli functor is representable — by the modular curve $Y(N)$. The automorphism obstruction has been eliminated by adding structure.

7. Preview: the fix is to target groupoids

The moral: if your objects have automorphisms, sets are the wrong target. The answer is to replace

$$\mathrm{CRing} \longrightarrow \mathsf{Set}$$

by

$$\mathrm{CRing} \longrightarrow \mathsf{Grpd},$$

sending $R$ to the groupoid of elliptic curves over $R$ with their isomorphisms. A lax-functorial, sheafy version of this is an algebraic stack. The scheme / functor-of-points story is the special case where every object has trivial automorphism groupoid.

Sketch. An algebraic stack is a category fibered in groupoids $\mathcal{X} \to \mathsf{Sch}$ satisfying a descent condition plus a representability / smoothness condition on the diagonal. Morphisms $S \to \mathcal{X}$ are not "elements of a set" but objects of the groupoid fiber $\mathcal{X}(S)$, and two such morphisms are equal when they are isomorphic. The isomorphisms are now encoded, so the sheaf condition returns.

The twist widget above is the key picture: the "twisted family" and the "product family" had the same classes at each point, but they are distinct objects of the groupoid fiber, because gluing by $[-1]$ is a different 2-arrow from gluing by $\mathrm{id}$. Upgrading the target category captures exactly that information.

The functor-of-points perspective is then the base camp from which stacks become a modest and natural extension — not a new abstraction, but the same game played in a 2-category.

Used in
Stacks · Groupoids, fibered categories, descent

8. The Yoneda embedding for schemes

The functor-of-points reframe of §1 is justified by a single theorem: the assignment

$$h_{(-)}: \mathsf{Sch} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fun}(\mathsf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathsf{Set}), \qquad X \longmapsto h_X = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(-, X)$$

is fully faithful. This is the Yoneda lemma in its representable form: for any presheaf $F$ on $\mathsf{Sch}$, $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, F) \cong F(X)$ naturally in $X$, with the isomorphism sending a natural transformation $\eta$ to $\eta_X(\mathrm{id}_X) \in F(X)$. Taking $F = h_Y$ yields $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X, h_Y) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sch}}(X,Y)$, so a natural transformation between representable functors is the same data as a scheme morphism — no more, no less.

Two consequences make this workhorse. First, to define a morphism $X \to Y$ you may equivalently give a rule that to each test ring $R$ assigns a function $X(R) \to Y(R)$ natural in $R$; second, to define a scheme at all it is sometimes cleaner to write down the functor $R \mapsto X(R)$ directly and then prove representability by exhibiting a representing scheme. The identity $\mathrm{id}_X \in h_X(X)$ is the universal element: every $R$-point $x \in X(R)$ is the pushforward of $\mathrm{id}_X$ along the classifying map $\mathrm{Spec}\,R \to X$, and every natural transformation out of $h_X$ is determined by its value there.

Why "embedding". Full faithfulness means $\mathsf{Sch}$ sits inside presheaves on rings as a reflective subcategory. Presheaves that land in that subcategory are exactly the representable ones — equivalently, those satisfying Zariski (or fppf, fpqc, …) descent plus a local representability condition. Objects that satisfy descent but are not representable are precisely the algebraic spaces and stacks from §7: the embedding picture tells you where the generalization lives.
See also
  • Universal properties and representability
</section>

9. Base change as pullback of functors

Representable functors preserve limits, so fiber products of schemes are computed pointwise on their functors of points:

$$(X \times_S Y)(T) \;=\; X(T) \times_{S(T)} Y(T).$$

Nothing about this equality is subtle — both sides are sets and it is an identity of sets, natural in the test scheme $T$. But the consequences for base change are immediate. Given $X \to S$ and a morphism $S' \to S$, the base-changed scheme $X_{S'} = X \times_S S'$ has functor of points $X_{S'}(T) = X(T) \times_{S(T)} S'(T)$; for affine $T = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$ and $S = \mathrm{Spec}\,k$ this simplifies to $X_k(R) = X(R)$ where $R$ is regarded with its given $k$-algebra structure. Extending scalars is literally restricting the functor to a slice category.

Three practical payoffs. (i) Computing fibers. The fiber $X_s$ of $X \to S$ over a point $s: \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s) \to S$ has $X_s(R) = \{x \in X(R) : x \text{ maps to } s\}$ — exactly solving the original equations over the residue field. (ii) Weil restriction. If $L/k$ is a finite extension and $X$ is an $L$-scheme, the Weil restriction $\mathrm{Res}_{L/k} X$ represents the functor $R \mapsto X(R \otimes_k L)$; the functor of points makes this definition, not a theorem. (iii) Twists and descent. Base-change agreement between two schemes over $L$ that become isomorphic over $\bar k$ is exactly equality of their functors restricted to $\bar k$-algebras, with Galois descent controlling when the common functor descends to a $k$-scheme.

Worked fiber. Let $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2 - p)$ with the obvious map to $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$. For a prime $\ell \ne p$ with $p$ a square mod $\ell$, the fiber $X_{\mathbb{F}_\ell}$ has $X_{\mathbb{F}_\ell}(\mathbb{F}_\ell) = \{\pm\sqrt{p}\} \subset \mathbb{F}_\ell$, two points; for $\ell = p$ it has the single fat point $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2)$. The functor of points reads these off without setting up any sheaves.
See also
  • Fiber products and base change
